rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is a severe but non-emergency acute flare of a KNOWN 10-year chronic plaque psoriasis. The presentation is alarming — severe skin pain, continuous bleeding from fissured plaques, temporary immobility, and an out-of-hours triage note that explicitly invokes erythroderma and sepsis — but the acute red-flag screen is NEGATIVE: the redness is in discrete plaques with normal intervening skin (not confluent whole-body erythema), there are no pustules and no purulent weeping, the patient is normothermic with no rigors and systemically well, and the bleeding is superficial fissure/Auspitz bleeding rather than haemorrhage. The source note's own in-person disposition is routine: topical treatment, urgent dermatology referral, and GP review in 3-5 days — not the emergency department. The correct telehealth answer is therefore T2 (prompt GP within 24-48 hours) with intensive flare management and an urgent (routine-urgent) dermatology referral, coupled with a PROMINENT MANDATORY safety-net escalating to T4/T5 for the emergency variants (erythroderma, generalised pustular psoriasis, secondary sepsis) that cannot be excluded remotely. Escalating this stable flare straight to ED (T4) or 000 (T5) on the strength of the pain and the out-of-hours framing is over-triage and is the primary scored defect; dismissing it to pure self-care with no review or safety-net is the under-triage defect.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Skin becomes bright red all over almost the whole body (confluent, not separate patches), and/or the patient cannot keep warm / feels burning hot — erythrodermic psoriasis
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Attend the emergency department immediately; call 000 if severely unwell, drowsy, or unable to keep warm
escalation_edges.rationale: Erythroderma is a dermatological emergency causing thermoregulatory failure, fluid/protein loss and cardiovascular strain; it needs urgent in-person and often inpatient management that telehealth cannot provide.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Right now the redness is in separate patches with normal skin between them, which is reassuring. If instead your skin turns bright red all over almost your whole body, or you can't keep warm or feel burning hot, go to the emergency department straight away — call 000 if you feel very unwell.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever with uncontrollable shivering/rigors, or thick yellow pus weeping from the cracked skin — secondary bacterial infection / staphylococcal sepsis
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek emergency care the same day; call 000 if there are signs of severe illness or shock
escalation_edges.rationale: Fissured, bleeding skin can become secondarily infected and progress to sepsis; fever with rigors and purulent discharge need urgent assessment and intravenous antibiotics that cannot be delivered on telehealth.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a high fever with uncontrollable shivering, or the cracks start weeping thick yellow pus, this could be a skin infection getting into your system — go to the emergency department the same day, and call 000 if you feel very unwell.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Crops of small pus-filled blisters spreading rapidly over reddened skin, with fever and feeling unwell — generalised pustular psoriasis
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Attend the emergency department the same day
escalation_edges.rationale: Generalised pustular psoriasis is an emergency variant requiring urgent in-person assessment and systemic therapy.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you suddenly get crops of little pus-filled blisters spreading over red skin along with a fever, don't wait — go to the emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Clusters of small blisters or punched-out sores spreading over the skin with fever — possible eczema herpeticum / herpetic superinfection
escalation_edges.rationale: A herpetic superinfection of diseased skin needs urgent antiviral treatment and in-person assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you notice clusters of small blisters or punched-out sores spreading across your skin, especially with a fever, get seen urgently in person the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The flare is not improving after starting emollient and topical steroid, pain remains uncontrolled, or the patient cannot cope with the immobility at home
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Bring the GP review forward and ensure the dermatology referral is actioned
escalation_edges.rationale: Ongoing severe symptoms without an emergency feature warrant earlier GP review, optimisation of treatment and expediting the specialist referral for systemic therapy — a prompt-care step, not an emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the tightness and cracking aren't easing within a couple of days of the greasy moisturiser and steroid ointment, or the pain isn't controlled, contact your GP sooner so they can review you and chase up the skin-specialist referral.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Bed-bound at home with severe pain and a spouse assisting with care, out of hours
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Care can safely begin at home now (emollient, analgesia, topical steroid) with the spouse's help, with prompt in-hours GP review arranged. Acknowledge the distress and genuine severity, but do NOT let the pain, the visible bleeding or the out-of-hours 'urgent' framing convert this stable flare into an emergency transfer — reserve after-hours/ED attendance for the safety-net triggers.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Temporary immobility from the painful fissured plaques limits the patient's ability to attend in person, particularly out of hours
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Favour home-initiated management plus a prompt in-hours GP review over an out-of-hours in-person trip for this stable flare. This access limitation must NOT, however, be used to withhold emergency escalation if a genuine safety-net trigger (erythroderma / sepsis / pustular flare) develops.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a severe flare of your known psoriasis. Start intensive greasy moisturiser over your whole body four times a day and after showering, apply the potent steroid ointment to the worst cracked patches, and take simple pain relief. Book a prompt GP appointment in the next day or two to review the flare and check for infection, and to make sure the urgent skin-specialist (dermatology) referral is actioned. The tightness and cracking should start easing within 24-48 hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Prompt GP appointment within 24-48 hours (or pharmacist for the emollient and over-the-counter pain relief in the meantime)
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This flare does not need after-hours emergency care by itself. Go to the emergency department (or call 000 if severely unwell) only if your skin turns bright red all over your whole body, you develop a high fever with uncontrollable shivering, crops of pus-filled blisters appear, the cracks weep thick yellow pus, or you cannot keep warm.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Adult with a 10-year history of plaque psoriasis presenting with a 7-day explosive flare after a recent streptococcal throat infection and severe stress: widespread thick silvery-scaled well-demarcated plaques over extensor elbows/knees, scalp, lower back and trunk, rigid and fissuring with painful bleeding and temporary immobility; a fresh Koebner plaque at a forearm scratch. On patient-obtainable data the plaques are discrete (not confluent whole-body erythema), there are no pustules, no purulent weeping, and the patient is normothermic and systemically well — no features of erythroderma, pustular psoriasis or secondary sepsis at presentation. Presumptive severe plaque psoriasis flare; please confirm on examination (Auspitz sign, BSA estimate, nail/pustule inspection), exclude the emergency variants and secondary infection, optimise topical therapy and analgesia, and expedite dermatology referral for systemic therapy / phototherapy.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot measure the exact body-surface-area involved, palpate the skin, or detect early pustulation or early secondary infection — so erythroderma, generalised pustular psoriasis and secondary sepsis cannot be definitively excluded remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Manage the flare actively at T2, be explicit that the current picture is reassuring (discrete plaques, no pustules, no fever), and hand the patient a prominent, specific, mandatory safety-net that names each emergency trigger and the exact action (ED / 000). Arrange prompt GP review to complete the in-person examination.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either resolving the uncertainty by escalating the whole stable flare to ED/000 now (over-triage), or ignoring it by reassuring the patient with no safety-net and no medical review (unsafe under-triage).

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the flare as a trivial rash with moisturiser only — no analgesia, no topical steroid, no medical review and no safety-net — so that a developing erythroderma, pustular flare or secondary sepsis could go unrecognised.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The patient deteriorates into an emergency variant or secondary sepsis at home without knowing the danger signs or having any review arranged.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Treating it as a routine 1-2 week problem without a prompt review and without communicating the emergency-variant safety-net, underplaying the severity of the flare and the specialist need.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating a stable, systemically-well severe plaque flare straight to the emergency department or calling 000, driven by the severe pain, the visible bleeding and the out-of-hours 'urgent' framing rather than by the (reassuring) clinical screen. This is the primary scored defect for this case.
